We found that the directional aspect of these quantities... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. full_notes:
Motion in a Straight Line is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that cHAPTER THREE MOTION IN A PLANE 3.1 INTRODUCTION In the last chapter we developed the concepts of position, displacement, velocity and acceleration that are needed to describe the motion of an object along a straight line.

The text explains that we found that the directional aspect of these quantities can be taken care of by + and – signs, as in one dimension only two directions are possible.

The text explains that but in order to describe motion of an object in two dimensions (a plane) or three dimensions (space), we need to use vectors to describe the above- mentioned physical quantities.

The text explains that therefore, it is first necessary to learn the language of vectors.

The text explains that what is the result of multiplying a vector by a real number ?

The text explains that we shall learn this to enable us to use vectors for defining velocity and acceleration in a plane.

The text explains that we then discuss motion of an object in a plane.

The text explains that as a simple case of motion in a plane, we shall discuss motion with constant acceleration and treat in detail the projectile motion.

The text explains that circular motion is a familiar class of motion that has a special significance in daily-life situations.

The text explains that we shall discuss uniform circular motion in some detail.

The text explains that the equations developed in this chapter for motion in a plane can be easily extended to the case of three dimensions.

The text explains that 3.2 SCALARS AND VECTORS In physics, we can classify quantities as scalars or vectors.

The text explains that basically, the difference is that a direction is associated with a vector but not with a scalar.

The text explains that a scalar quantity is a quantity with magnitude only.

The text explains that it is specified completely by a single number, along with the proper unit.

The text explains that examples are : the distance between two points, mass of an object, the temperature of a body and the time at which a certain event happened.

The text explains that take a n o t h e r e x a m p l e : t h e m a x i m u m a n d minimum temperatures on a particular day are 35.6 °C and 24.2 °C respectively.

The text explains that then, the difference between the two temperatures is 11.4 °C.

The text explains that similarly, if a uniform solid cube of aluminium of side 10 cm has a mass of 2.7 kg, then its volume is 10–3 m3 (a scalar) and its density is 2.7×103 kg m–3 (a scalar).

The text explains that a vector quantity is a quantity that has both a magnitude and a direction and obeys the triangle law of addition or equivalently the parallelogram law of addition.

The text explains that so, a vector is specified by giving its magnitude by a number and its direction.

The text explains that the length of the vector r represents the magnitude of the vector and its direction is the direction in which P lies as seen from O.

The text explains that this fact was emphasised in the previous chapter also while discussing motion along a straight line.

The text explains that 3.2.2 Equality of Vectors Two vectors A and B are said to be equal if, and only if, they have the same magnitude and the same direction.** Figure 3.2(a) shows two equal vectors A and B.

The text explains that 3.2(b), vectors A′ and B′ have the same magnitude but they are not equal because they have different directions.

The text explains that 3.3 MULTIPLICATION OF VECTORS BY REAL NUMBERS Multiplying a vector A with a positive number λ gives a vector whose magnitude is changed by the factor λ but the direction is the same as that of A : λ A = λ A if λ > 0.

The text explains that for example, if A is multiplied by 2, the resultant vector 2A is in the same direction as A and has a magnitude twice of |A| as shown in Fig.

The text explains that multiplying a vector A by a negative number −λ gives another vector whose direction is opposite to the direction of A and whose magnitude is λ times |A|.

The text explains that since the magnitudes of the two vectors are the same, but the directions are opposite, the resultant vector has zero magnitude and is represented by 0 called a null vector or a zero vector : A – A = 0 |0|= 0 (3.3) Since the magnitude of a null vector is zero, its direction cannot be specified.

The text explains that the resultant vector R is directed from the common origin O along the diagonal (OS) of the parallelogram [Fig.

The text explains that winds starts blowing after sometime with a speed of 12 m s–1 in east to west direction.

The text explains that in which direction should a boy waiting at a bus stop hold his umbrella ?

The text explains that 3.7 and are in the direction specified by the problem.

The text explains that the magnitude of R is R v v r 2 w 2        35 12 m s 37 m s 2 2 1 1 The direction q that R makes with the vertical is given by 12 tan 0.343 35 w r v v     Or,       tan- .

The text explains that 3.5 RESOLUTION OF VECTORS Let a and b be any two non-zero vectors in a plane with different directions and let A be another vector in the same plane (Fig.

The text explains that it is convenient to resolve a general vector along the axes of a rectangular coordinate system using vectors of unit magnitude.

The text explains that a unit vector is a vector of unit magnitude and points in a particular direction.

The text explains that unit vectors along the x-, y- and z-axes of a rectangular coordinate system are denoted by ɵi , ɵj and ˆk , respectively, as shown in Fig.

The text explains that since we are dealing with motion in two dimensions in this chapter, we require use of only two unit vectors.

The text explains that it can be specified by : (i) its magnitude A and the direction θ it makes with the x-axis; or (ii) its components Ax and Ay If A and θ are given, Ax and Ay can be obtained using Eq.

The text explains that example 3.2 Find the magnitude and direction of the resultant of two vectors A and B in terms of their magnitudes and angle θ between them.

The text explains that (3.24d) as the Law of sines. ⊳ Example 3.3 A motorboat is racing towards north at 25 km/h and the water current in that region is 10 km/h in the direction of 60° east of south.

The text explains that using the parallelogram method of addition, the resultant R is obtained in the direction shown in the figure.

The text explains that 3.11 We can obtain the magnitude of R using the Law of cosine :    2 2 b b b c 2 cos120 R v v v v = 25 10 2 25 10 -1/2 22 km/h 2 2 + + × × ( ) ≅ To obtain the direction, we apply the Law of sines R vc sin sin    or, sin    v R c sin  10 sin120 21.8 10 3 2 21.8 0.397        23.4 ⊳ 3.7 MOTION IN A PLANE In this section we shall see how to describe motion in two dimensions using vectors.

The text explains that then, the displacement is : ∆r = r′ – r (3.25) and is directed from P to P′.

The text explains that (3.25) in a component form: ∆r ( ) ( ) = + − + x' y' x ɵ ɵ ɵ ɵ i j i j y = + ɵ ɵ i j ∆ ∆ x y where ∆x = x ′ – x, ∆y = y′ – y (3.26) Velocity The average velocity ( ) v of an object is the ratio of the displacement and the corresponding time interval : v r i j i j = = + = + ∆ ∆ ∆ ∆ ∆ ∆ ∆ ∆ ∆ t x y t x t y t ɵ ɵ ɵ ɵ (3.27) Or,  ˆ x y v v = + v i j Since v r = ∆ ∆t , the direction of the average velocity is the same as that of ∆r (Fig.

The text explains that the velocity (instantaneous velocity) is given by the limiting value of the average velocity as the time interval approaches zero : v r r = = → lim t t t∆ ∆ ∆ 0 d d (3.28) The meaning of the limiting process can be easily understood with the help of Fig 3.13(a) to (d).

The text explains that (b) What is the magnitude and direction of velocity of the particle at t = 2.0 s ?

The text explains that 3.18 A particle starts from the origin at t = 0 s with a velocity of 10.0 jɵ m/s and moves in the x-y plane with a constant acceleration of ( ) 8.0 2.0 ɵ ɵ i j + m s-2.

The text explains that (a) At what time is the x- coordinate of the particle 16 m?

The text explains that what is the y-coordinate of the particle at that time?

The text explains that (b) What is the speed of the particle at the time ?

Source: NCERT Class 11 Physics, Chapter 3: Motion in a Straight Line
